quiz.title: Unit 3 Quiz 8, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Ionic Radius Comparisons and Electron Affinity Trends
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Cation vs Atom Size, Anion vs Atom Size, Electron Affinity Definition, Periodic Trends in Electron Affinity, Anomalies (Fluorine vs Chlorine), Noble Gas Electron Affinity

questions.question: Which trend is correct?
questions.explanation: Cations are smaller than their neutral atoms because losing electrons reduces electron-electron repulsion and often removes an entire electron shell. Anions are larger than atoms, so option B is incorrect.

questions.question: Ionic radius is measured in:
questions.explanation: Ionic radius is typically measured in picometers (pm) or Angstroms (Å). 1 pm = 10⁻¹² m. Joules measure energy, Coulombs measure charge.

questions.question: Why are cations smaller than their atoms?
questions.explanation: Cations have fewer electrons than their neutral atoms, which reduces electron-electron repulsion. Often, the entire outer shell is lost, resulting in a significantly smaller radius.

questions.question: Why are anions larger than their atoms?
questions.explanation: Anions have additional electrons, which increases electron-electron repulsion and causes the electron cloud to expand, making the ion larger than the neutral atom.

questions.question: Electron affinity is best defined as:
questions.explanation: Electron affinity is the energy released when an atom in the gaseous state accepts an electron to form a negative ion. (Option A is ionization energy, option C is electronegativity.)

questions.question: Electron affinity generally becomes more negative:
questions.explanation: Electron affinity generally becomes more negative (more energy released) across a period from left to right as atoms gain protons and have a stronger pull on additional electrons.

questions.question: Which element has the highest electron affinity?
questions.explanation: Chlorine actually has the highest (most negative) electron affinity (-349 kJ/mol). Fluorine has high electron affinity but is slightly lower than chlorine due to its small size causing electron-electron repulsion in the small 2p subshell.

questions.question: Which group has the lowest electron affinity?
questions.explanation: Noble gases have the lowest (or even positive) electron affinities because they have full valence shells. Adding an electron would require placing it in a higher energy level, which is energetically unfavorable.

questions.question: Electron affinity is usually expressed in:
questions.explanation: Electron affinity is commonly expressed in electron volts (eV) per atom or kilojoules per mole (kJ/mol). The question likely expects electron volts as the unit of energy at the atomic scale.

questions.question: Noble gases have low electron affinity because they:
questions.explanation: Noble gases have full valence electron shells (octet, except helium). Adding an electron would require starting a new shell at a higher energy level, which is energetically unfavorable, resulting in low or positive electron affinity.

questions.question: Which element has a more negative electron affinity?
questions.explanation: Chlorine has a very negative electron affinity (-349 kJ/mol). Sodium and magnesium have low or positive values, and argon has a positive electron affinity (unfavorable to add an electron).

questions.question: Across a period, electron affinity generally:
questions.explanation: Electron affinity generally increases (becomes more negative) across a period as nuclear charge increases, making the atom more attractive to an additional electron.

questions.question: Which element has near-zero electron affinity?
questions.explanation: Neon, like other noble gases, has a near-zero or positive electron affinity because its valence shell is full. Adding an electron would require entering a higher energy level, which is not energetically favorable.

questions.question: Which factor increases electron affinity?
questions.explanation: Stronger nuclear pull (higher effective nuclear charge) increases electron affinity by attracting an additional electron more strongly, releasing more energy when it is added.

questions.question: Which element has lower electron affinity than expected?
questions.explanation: Fluorine has a slightly lower electron affinity than chlorine despite being more electronegative. This is because fluorine's small size causes significant electron-electron repulsion when adding an electron to its compact 2p subshell.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered ionic radius comparisons and electron affinity trends! ⚛️📊
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the fluorine-chlorine electron affinity anomaly and noble gas exceptions.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on cation vs anion size differences and the definition of electron affinity.
scoreMessages.0: Please review ionic radius trends, electron affinity definitions, and periodic patterns.
